project_title_fr: Système de reconnaissance faciale du programme de Passeport
description_en:
The Passport Program (formerly Passport Canada) began using facial recognition technology in 2010 to support identity authentication, help detect fraud, and prevent passport and travel document issuance to ineligible applicants. 
Facial recognition technology is a tool that detects and analyzes the facial features of a person’s face for the purpose of assisting in the identification and verification of an individual’s identity. A facial recognition system converts facial images into facial biometric templates that may be stored within the system and used for future comparison.    The system can compare a facial biometric against a collection of other facial biometrics resulting in a list of candidates ranked by a computer evaluated similarity score; this is called a one-to-many (1:N) identification. It can also conduct a verification by comparing a facial biometric to another facial biometric in the same claimed identity; this is called a one-to-one (1:1) verification.
For passport purposes, three comparisons are performed. First, all adult applicant facial biometrics are compared to a database of biometric templates from past passport applicants to determine if the person has previously applied for a passport and to help detect passport and travel document related fraud.  Similarly all adult applicants are compared to a collection of templates of individuals whose entitlement to a Canadian passport or travel document warrants additional review (e.g. individuals who have previously misrepresented their identity). Finally, for returning applicants, the Passport Program's facial recognition system compares the applicant's facial biometric to previous templates in the same identity (i.e. previous passport records) to verify they are the same person.
description_fr:
Le Programme de passeport (anciennement Passeport Canada) a commencé à utiliser la technologie de reconnaissance faciale en 2010 pour appuyer l’authentification de l’identité, aider à détecter la fraude et prévenir la délivrance de passeports et de titres de voyage à des requérants inadmissibles.
La technologie de reconnaissance faciale est un outil qui détecte et analyse les traits physionomiques d’une personne en vue d’aider à l’identification d’une personne et à la vérification de son identité. Un système de reconnaissance faciale convertit les images faciales en des modèles biométriques faciaux qui peuvent être stockés dans le système et utilisés ultérieurement aux fins de comparaison. Le système peut comparer les données biométriques faciales d’une personne à un ensemble d’autres données biométriques faciales pour ainsi obtenir une liste de candidats classés selon une cote de similarité évaluée par ordinateur : c’est ce qu’on appelle une identification « un à plusieurs » (1:N). Le système peut également procéder à une vérification en comparant les données biométriques faciales d’une personne à d’autres données biométriques faciales associées à la même identité revendiquée : c’est ce qu’on appelle une vérification « un à un » (1:1).
Pour les besoins du Programme de passeport, trois types de comparaisons sont effectuées. D’abord, les données biométriques faciales de l’ensemble des requérants adultes sont comparées à tous les modèles biométriques d’anciens requérants de passeport contenus dans une base de données pour déterminer si une personne a déjà présenté une demande de passeport et aider à détecter la fraude liée aux passeports et aux titres de voyage. De même, les données de tous les requérants adultes sont comparées à un ensemble de modèles associés à des personnes dont l’admissibilité à un passeport ou à un titre de voyage canadien doit faire l’objet d’un examen supplémentaire (p. ex., les personnes qui ont déjà fait de fausses déclarations sur leur identité). Enfin, dans le cas des requérants ayant déjà présenté une demande par le passé, le système de reconnaissance faciale du Programme de passeport compare les données biométriques faciales du requérant à d’anciens modèles associés à la même identité (c. à d. les anciens dossiers de passeport) pour vérifier qu’il s’agit bien de la même personne.

{"projectDetailsRespondent": "Sean McNair", "projectDetailsJob": "Director General", "projectDetailsDepartment-NS": "050", "projectDetailsBranch": "Visitors and Identity Management Branch", "projectDetailsTitle": "Passport Program Facial Recognition System", "projectDetailsID": "N/A", "projectDetailsProgram": "Passport Program", "projectDetailsPhase": "item2", "projectDetailsDescription": "The Passport Program (formerly Passport Canada) began using facial recognition technology in 2010 to support identity authentication, help detect fraud, and prevent passport and travel document issuance to ineligible applicants. 
Facial recognition technology is a tool that detects and analyzes the facial features of a person’s face for the purpose of assisting in the identification and verification of an individual’s identity. A facial recognition system converts facial images into facial biometric templates that may be stored within the system and used for future comparison.    The system can compare a facial biometric against a collection of other facial biometrics resulting in a list of candidates ranked by a computer evaluated similarity score; this is called a one-to-many (1:N) identification. It can also conduct a verification by comparing a facial biometric to another facial biometric in the same claimed identity; this is called a one-to-one (1:1) verification.
For passport purposes, three comparisons are performed. First, all adult applicant facial biometrics are compared to a database of biometric templates from past passport applicants to determine if the person has previously applied for a passport and to help detect passport and travel document related fraud.  Similarly all adult applicants are compared to a collection of templates of individuals whose entitlement to a Canadian passport or travel document warrants additional review (e.g. individuals who have previously misrepresented their identity). Finally, for returning applicants, the Passport Program's facial recognition system compares the applicant's facial biometric to previous templates in the same identity (i.e. previous passport records) to verify they are the same person.
", "businessDrivers1": ["item2", "item3", "item4", "item5"], "businessDrivers3": "Clients want a secure, globally respected travel document that will facilitate domestic and international travel. Canadian passport holders enjoy visa-free access to most countries due, in part, to Canada's reputation of having strong control measures, like the use of facial recognition technology, in place. Clients surveyed indicate they want a faster and simpler passport application process that includes the option to apply for a passport online.  The use of facial recognition technology supports automated processing to meet increasing service demand and will enable eligible clients to apply to renew their passport online.  ", "businessDrivers4": "The Program's use of facial recognition technology gives the Canada Border Services Agency and other government agencies assurance that the travel document holder is the owner of the identity presented and that they hold Canadian citizenship, or Refugee, Protected Person, or Permanent Resident status in Canada.   The system helps support an online service channel option and automated processing making the application process faster and simpler for most clients.  ", "businessDrivers5": "item3-0", "businessDrivers6": "The facial recognition system has enabled the Passport Program to advance modernization initiatives while maintaining program integrity. This includes a simplified renewal application process and automated decision-making for routine non-complex applications that result in a positive decision. (i.e. approximately 80% of adult passport renewals).", "businessDrivers7": "Access to the system is controlled and limited to use by IRCC's Passport Program to help make determinations about a persons current, ongoing, and future entitlement to a Canadian travel document.", "businessDrivers8": "It is possible that the use of facial biometrics may engage a clients reasonable expectation of privacy. With this in mind, the program completed the following four-part test, as recommended by the Office of the Privacy Commission, to assess whether  the use of facial recognition technology is justifiable when weighed against the prospective privacy concern.
Test 1. Is the measure demonstrably necessary to meet a specific need? Yes, the Passport Program is mandated with issuing secure globally respected travel documents to facilitate domestic and international travel. The strength of the passport rests on the security of the document itself and the integrity of passport entitlement processes. Use of facial recognition helps support sound identity assessments which are essential to meet the objective of only issuing passports to those who are eligible 
Test 2. Is it likely to be effective in meeting that need? Yes, the facial recognition system effectively meets the need to support sound identity and entitlement assessments.  Performance tests of the system has showed the technology exhibited a high degree of accuracy by correctly matching pairs of faces of the same person. 
Test 3. Would the loss of privacy be proportionate to the benefit gained? Yes. The conversion of a photo into a biometric template for facial recognition comparison allows us to have more confidence in the identity of our clients, which allows us to offer convenient service options like the simplified renewal application process and will enable eligible clients to renew their passport online.  The use of facial recognition strengthens identity management processes and helps protect client identities from being used by another person to obtain a passport.  
Test 4. Is there a less privacy-invasive way of achieving the same end? No. While employees are trained to visually compare the likeness of applicants to their previous passport photos (one-to-one verification), it is impossible for them to compare each applicants face to all other applicant faces (one-to-many identification). Studies also show that facial recognition technology, is not only faster, but also superior to humans at face matching.
The biometrics community recognizes that the performance of facial recognition technology can vary depending on factors like image quality, age, gender and skin tone.  Using high quality images along with a high performing algorithm improves accuracy in general and across diverse populations.  IRCC's Passport Program requires that photos are taken by a commercial photographer and must meet international standards. The Program also uses an algorithm that has proven to perform well when tested by the National Institute for Standard in Technology (NIST).  False matches that may be produced by the system are quickly adjudicated and returned to regular processing within established service standards.
", "businessDrivers9": "item1-0", "businessDrivers10": "A non-automated process may be completed by humans who visually compare the client's passport photo to photos associated to previous applications in the same identity to assess their similarity. The facial recognition system can complete this comparison (one-to-one verification) and can also compare the clients face to other clients faces to assess their similarity and help flag  possible instances of client's who are already known by another identity (one-to-many identification). The latter comparison is not feasible using a non-automated process.", "businessDrivers11": ["item2-2", "item4-0", "item6-0"], "businessDrivers12": "Integrity of the passport issuance process is not as high.", "riskProfile1": "item1-3", "riskProfile2": "item2-0", "riskProfile3": "item2-0", "riskProfile4": "item2-0", "riskProfile5": "item2-0", "projectAuthority1": "item2-0", "aboutSystem1": ["item1"], "aboutAlgorithm1": "item1-3", "aboutAlgorithm2": "item2-0", "decisionSector3": "The system compares the client's facial biometric to previous facial biometric templates in the same identity to assess whether the client appears to be same person who applied for passport services in this identity before to help verify they are who they say they are. The system also compares the likeness of the clients facial biometric to facial biometrics associated to other identities that are held in the facial recognition system to assess whether the client may be known to the Passport Program under another identity. If the system determines that the client appears to be the same person who used this identity before and they do not appear to be known by another identity, the application is automatically returned to continue processing.    Facial recognition is just one of many checks that are completed as part of the passport issuance process and facial recognition results are never the sole factor that determines whether a client may be issued a passport or travel document.", "decisionSector1": ["item6-1"], "decisionSector2": "Canadian passport and travel document services", "impact30": "item2-2", "impact31": "The system plays a role in identity and entitlement assessments by converting passport photos into facial biometrics and comparing them to other facial biometrics held in the facial recognition system. These comparisons help to determine whether the client may have previously applied for a passport in the same identity or another identity, and whether their entitlement to a passport may warrant further review. The system produces a numeric value that represents the similarity score of the facial biometrics being compared (i.e. how similar or dissimilar are the faces). The score either falls within (pass) or outside (fail) a set threshold that is also represented by a numeric value. Only cases that pass all facial recognition comparison checks may be fully automated. All cases that fail a facial recognition comparison are referred to employees for assessment and decision. The system is just one of many integrity tools in place and does not render a final decision to approve or refuse passport issuance or to revoke a passport.", "impact3": "item2-0", "impact20": "Client photos are the primary data source. Passport photos must be taken by a commercial photographer within 6 months of applying for a passport and must capture the person's neutral facial expression. Only photos that meet specifications based on international standards are acceptable. The combination of these requirements (recent, high quality, standardized) minimizes the risk that outdated or unreliable data is used. Passport officers are trained to review the photo for acceptability and the facial recognition system has a built in feature to assess photo compatibility.", "impact21": "The system assigns a numeric score to reflect how similar or dissimilar two faces are. Scores that meet a set similarity threshold,  as represented by a numeric value, are automatically passed and returned to processing. A score that does not meet a set similarity threshold is referred for human review and decision. As part of the first review, the human operator is shown the two faces in question and asked to decide if they depict the same person or a different person. The system produced score is not shown to the operator to avoid adding bias to their decision. This operator is unaware of which type of comparison generated the failed case and does not know whether the two faces are supposed to be the same person or a different person. If the operator independently reaches the same decision as the system, the case is automatically escalated to a higher level of review where operators are able to access the system generated similarity score and information that would appear on the associated travel document/application (personalization and issuance details) to help adjudicate the case. If the next operator agrees that the case is problematic it is escalated to another operator for review. The facial recognition system never makes a decision to issue or refuse to issue a travel document. All decisions to refuse to issue a travel document, withhold service or revoke a travel document are made by a human and never based solely on the result of a facial recognition comparison result.", "impact22": "item1-2", "impact23": "The system uses a different method than humans to compare faces; facial biometric versus visual inspection. The system also compares the client's face to millions of biometrics in other identities (e.g. all other adult passport applicants) which cannot be completed by a human.", "impact5": "item2-0", "impact6": "item1-1", "impact8": "Impacts can last years. While the facial recognition system does not render a decision to issue or not issue a passport, its decision is one component that feeds into automated approval processes. The impact of a decision to issue a passport can last years given passports are valid for up to ten years and may be eligible for renewal.", "impact9": "item1-1", "impact10": "The system does not make a decision to issue or refuse to issue a travel document and therefore its decision will have little to no impact on the rights or freedoms of individuals.
